label: ai
topic: How to start unit testing JavaScript code
text:
Writing JavaScript can sometimes feel like building a delicate tower of playing cards. You spend hours perfectly balancing a new feature, only to realize that tweaking a completely unrelated function has caused your entire application to collapse. This pervasive fear of breaking existing code is exactly why developers rely on automated testing. By writing code that tests your code, you create a permanent safety net that catches errors before they ever reach your users.

To understand testing, you must first understand the three main layers of the testing pyramid: unit tests, integration tests, and functional tests. Functional tests, often called end-to-end tests, sit at the very top of the pyramid. They simulate a real user clicking through your app in a browser to ensure the entire system works from start to finish. Integration tests sit in the middle, checking to see if multiple pieces of your application communicate correctly with one another, such as a database talking to a user interface.

Unit tests sit at the broad base of the pyramid and are the best place for beginners to start. A unit test focuses on the smallest possible part of your code—usually a single, isolated function. The goal is to provide that function with specific inputs and verify that it returns the exact output you expect. Because they are so small and focused, unit tests are incredibly fast to run. They isolate bugs down to the exact line of code, serving as both a diagnostic tool and a living documentation of what your code is supposed to do.

If you want to start writing unit tests in JavaScript, the most popular and approachable framework to use is Jest. Created by Facebook, Jest is beloved because it requires almost no configuration to get started and includes everything you need right out of the box.

Here is how you can write your very first unit test using Jest.

1. Ensure you have Node.js installed on your computer, then open your terminal and navigate to your project folder.
2. Initialize a new Node project by typing `npm init -y` in the terminal.
3. Install the Jest framework by running the command `npm install --save-dev jest`.
4. Open your `package.json` file and change the "test" script to read `"test": "jest"`.
5. Create a new JavaScript file named `math.js` and write a simple function, like `function add(a, b) { return a + b; }`. Export this function at the bottom of the file.
6. Create a companion testing file named `math.test.js`. Jest automatically looks for any files ending in `.test.js` to execute.
7. Inside your test file, import your add function. Use Jest's `test` function to describe what you are doing, writing something like `test('adds 1 + 2 to equal 3', () => { ... })`.
8. Inside that test block, use Jest's `expect` assertion. You will write `expect(add(1, 2)).toBe(3);`.
9. Finally, return to your terminal and type `npm test`.

You will see a wonderful green checkmark appear on your screen, indicating that your code works exactly as intended. As your codebase grows, you can write hundreds of these tests. Running them takes mere seconds, providing you with the ultimate confidence that your new features haven't broken your old ones.
